stdout:
**Content Standards**

Blocking failure: all six implementation issues have unmarked success criteria. The canonical standard says that when the planning-bracket coverage model is in use, “prefix every criterion with `[hard]` or `[aspirational]` and a stable `REQ-N` id.” These issues use plain checkboxes only, e.g. `04559558` has `- [ ] .jit/templates.toml parses...`; `643874f2`, `3143ad87`, `96fc8cd8`, `adc640e4`, and `99a0c161` have the same defect.

Concrete fix: rewrite each criteria list to add stable per-issue IDs, for example:

- `04559558`: `[hard] REG-01` through `[hard] REG-04`
- `643874f2`: `[hard] APPLY-01` through `[hard] APPLY-04`
- `3143ad87`: `[hard] SCAFFOLD-01` through `[hard] SCAFFOLD-04`
- `96fc8cd8`: `[hard] CONFIG-01` through `[hard] CONFIG-04`
- `adc640e4`: `[hard] DOCS-01` through `[hard] DOCS-03`
- `99a0c161`: `[hard] TEST-01` through `[hard] TEST-03`

For example, `04559558` should become:

```markdown
## Success Criteria
- [ ] [hard] REG-01: `.jit/templates.toml` parses into typed templates; an invalid template fails at config load with a clear error.
- [ ] [hard] REG-02: The `plan` template is authored and round-trips through the loader.
- [ ] [hard] REG-03: The registry yields, for a container type, the applicable template and its planning/breakdown node types, doc location, and breakable types.
- [ ] [hard] REG-04: Existing behavior is unchanged this wave.
```

The same mechanical rewrite is needed on the other five issues.

Minor note: titles are clean for the implementation issues. Membership labels use `epic:graph-templates`, which is a valid kebab slug.

**Coverage vs Design**

| Design item | Issue | Matches? |
|---|---:|---|
| W1 — Template registry, `.jit/templates.toml` schema/loader, repo `plan` template, additive accessors | `04559558` | Yes |
| W2 — Apply engine, interpolation, anchor wiring, `move-upstream-to-role` | `643874f2` | Yes |
| W3 — Add `jit apply`, switch scaffold atomically, breakdown consumes pre-created B, remove old commands | `3143ad87` | Yes |
| W4 — Migrate remaining `PlanningConfig` consumers, remove flat config and examples | `96fc8cd8` | Yes |
| W5 — Migrate skills, memory, docs, bracket id convention | `adc640e4` | Yes |
| W6 — Comprehensive tests, dogfood, reseed old empty planning descriptions | `99a0c161` | Mostly yes |

Blocking failure: W6 wrongly merges two differently scoped items: REQ-08 automated tests/dogfood and REQ-09 manual reseeding of existing planning-node descriptions. The design explicitly says REQ-09 is “manual (not engine)” and “not an engine deliverable,” while `99a0c161` is titled “Tests and dogfood for the graph-template engine” but also includes “The existing empty planning-node descriptions are reseeded.” This makes manual data cleanup part of the engine test task.

Concrete fix: split REQ-09 into its own task:

```bash
jit issue create --type task --title "Reseed existing empty planning-node descriptions" --label epic:graph-templates --label milestone:v1.0 --label satisfies:REQ-09
jit dep add <new-reseed-task> 96fc8cd8
jit dep rm 99a0c161 adc640e4
jit dep add 99a0c161 96fc8cd8
```

Then remove the reseed criterion and `satisfies:REQ-09` from `99a0c161`. If dogfood truly needs migrated skills, add a separate docs/skill dogfood criterion to `adc640e4`; do not serialize all engine tests behind W5.

**Dependency DAG Correctness**

Blocking failure: the implemented graph is a single chain:

`99a0c161 → adc640e4 → 96fc8cd8 → 3143ad87 → 643874f2 → 04559558 → 59118ee9 → 7d88e37d`

The design’s “Decomposition” section says W6 depends on “W1–W4; W5 for skill-level dogfood.” The actual edge `99a0c161` depends on `adc640e4`, which forces all comprehensive tests, property tests, and manual reseeding to wait for skills/docs migration. That is an over-constraint: most of `99a0c161` only needs W4.

Concrete fix:

```bash
jit dep rm 99a0c161 adc640e4
jit dep add 99a0c161 96fc8cd8
```

Then either move skill-level dogfood into `adc640e4`, or split it into a small task that depends on both `adc640e4` and `99a0c161`.

Blank-workspace reachability: the only implementation root is `04559558`, and it can start on a fresh workspace after the breakdown/planning bracket. No missing structural edge found at the root.

**Structural Integrity**

Blocking failure: this is a large epic but the breakdown is a flat set of six `type:task` leaves chained directly under the epic. The review standard requires decomposition depth to suit work size, specifically “large work is multi-level — epic → story → task — not a flat layer of leaves.” The approved plan covers registry, engine, CLI migration, config removal, skills/docs, and test/dogfood work; each wave contains multiple concrete work items. The current issue set has no `type:story` layer and no child tasks under the wave-level items.

Concrete fix: convert the six wave issues into `type:story` issues, give each a `story:<kebab-slug>` label, and create child tasks for the actual implementation pieces. Minimum story labels:

```bash
jit issue update 04559558 --remove-label type:task --label type:story --label story:template-registry
jit issue update 643874f2 --remove-label type:task --label type:story --label story:apply-engine
jit issue update 3143ad87 --remove-label type:task --label type:story --label story:scaffold-switch
jit issue update 96fc8cd8 --remove-label type:task --label type:story --label story:planning-config-removal
jit issue update adc640e4 --remove-label type:task --label type:story --label story:skills-docs-migration
jit issue update 99a0c161 --remove-label type:task --label type:story --label story:template-testing-dogfood
```

Then add child tasks under each story matching the plan bullets. For example, under `3143ad87`: “Add apply CLI dispatch”, “Refactor breakdown to consume existing breakdown node”, “Remove old planning scaffold commands”, and “Port scaffold and breakdown tests.”

**Prior Review Feedback**

`run_history` is empty, so there is no prior breakdown-review feedback to check.

VERDICT: FAIL
